section: Research on the microstructure and corrosion behaviour of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel alloyed with Cu

context_update.summary: This chunk contains the Abstract and Introduction of the paper. The Abstract summarizes key findings on Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-xCu (x=0,1,3 wt%) low-density steels: appropriate Cu addition promotes austenite nucleation and growth while refining carbides, whereas excessive Cu sharply reduces carbide number; Cu accumulates as elemental Cu at grain boundaries, creating a potential difference that synergizes with carbides to cause preferential grain boundary corrosion. The Introduction reviews prior work on Cu effects in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, including strengthening by Cu-rich precipitates, enhanced hydrogen embrittlement resistance, refined twins and higher dislocation density improving plasticity and strength, and tensile properties of Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu steel (UTS 1026.7 MPa, TE 43.1%, YS 926.4 MPa). It also highlights Cu's role in inhibiting κ-carbide dissolution and promoting intergranular κ-carbides. Table 1 gives the full chemical compositions of the three experimental steels, all containing 0.2Si, 0.5W, 0.5Cr, 0.5Mo, 0.5Nb (wt%). No processing route is detailed yet. The expected follow-up involves experimental procedures and results linking Cu content to microstructure and corrosion behavior.
material_systems: Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic light steel (from Ren et al.)
Cu-alloyed dual-phase light steel (from Song et al.)
microstructure: fine twins and high dislocation density in Cu-containing steel (from Xie et al.)
B2 phase segregation with Cu (from Song et al.)
properties: Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu: UTS 1026.7 MPa, TE 43.1%, YS 926.4 MPa (Ren et al.)
improved plasticity and yield strength via deformation twins and dislocations (Xie et al.)
enhanced hydrogen embrittlement resistance (Song et al., Wang et al.)
mechanisms: microgalvanic corrosion between elemental Cu at grain boundaries and austenite
synergistic corrosion of Cu and carbides causing preferential grain boundary attack
Cu-rich precipitation strengthening
hydrogen trapping and barrier effect of Cu/B2 segregation

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the tail of the Introduction motivating the Cu effect study and the full Experimental section. The materials are Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C with 0, 1, or 3 wt% Cu; they were vacuum melted, Ar-cast, homogenized at 1200 °C/2 h, multipass hot rolled above 850 °C, and air cooled. Microstructure characterization methods are detailed: SEM on 10x10x5 mm samples etched in 20% nitric alcohol, EDS averaging 5 positions; XRD from 20° to 90° 2θ (40 kV, 40 mA, 2°/min); TEM foils prepared by double-jet polishing (10% perchloric acid / 90% methanol, −30 °C, 20 V, 200 mA). Electrochemical tests use a three-electrode cell with SCE, Pt counter electrode, 3.5 wt% NaCl at 25 °C, OCP stabilization for 1800 s, EIS at OCP (10 mV, 100 kHz–10 mHz, Zview fitting), and potentiodynamic polarization at 0.5 mV/s from −1.2 to −1.0 V vs. SCE with Tafel fitting. Immersion tests run for 5 days in 3.5 wt% NaCl with daily solution change, post-ultrasonic cleaning weight measurement, and OM observation. Figure 1 shows SEM images and XRD patterns; Figure 2 reports ASTM grain size numbers: 0Cu = 10.2, 3Cu = 9.6. Subsequent chunks should provide detailed micrographs, phase identification, electrochemical parameters (Ecorr, Icorr, impedance fits), and corrosion morphology/rate results.
key_variables: EIS amplitude: 10 mV, frequency: 100 kHz – 10 mHz
polarization scan rate: 0.5 mV/s, potential range: −1.2 to −1.0 V vs. SCE
microstructure: SEM images and XRD patterns presented in Figure 1

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the SKPFM method (2.5) and the first part of Results (3.1) on microstructure and composition. Quantitative grain size statistics from Fig. 2 are provided: ASTM grain size numbers 10.2 (0Cu), 9.5 (1Cu), and 9.6 (3Cu), demonstrating that Cu promotes austenite growth. Carbide populations are quantified via MPAR image analysis (Fig. 4): 0Cu has mean carbide size 0.52 μm and 1740 carbides; 1Cu refines carbides (0.51 μm, 2377 count); 3Cu drastically reduces carbide count to 876 while the size is not textually reported. EPMA element maps (Fig. 3) identify white particles as NbC‑type carbides in 1Cu and Cu enrichment at grain boundaries in 3Cu, with uneven Al distribution in 3Cu. XRD confirms a single γ-austenite phase. The potentiodynamic polarization curves are presented in Fig. 5 without extracted parameters. The SKPFM setup is described but no Volta potential data are given. The next chunks should deliver electrochemical parameters, immersion corrosion results, SKPFM potential maps, and mechanistic interpretation linking Cu segregation, carbide distribution, and corrosion behavior.
microstructure: equiaxed austenite with twins and white particles
white particles are NbC carbides in 1Cu and elemental Cu + remnant NbC in 3Cu
Cu segregation at austenite grain boundaries (slight in 1Cu, strong in 3Cu)
uneven Al distribution at grain boundaries in 3Cu
mechanisms: Cu in 0.52–3 wt% promotes austenite nucleation and growth
1 wt% Cu refines carbides and increases their number; 3 wt% Cu drastically reduces carbide amount, likely by hindering C diffusion

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the carbide quantification using MPAR (Fig. 4), providing mean carbide size and count for 0Cu and 1Cu, and carbide count for 3Cu, along with a literature-based description of Cu's effect on carbide refinement at low Cu levels and suppression at higher Cu. Electrochemical characterization begins with potentiodynamic polarization in 3.5% NaCl (Section 3.2.1, Fig. 5, Table 2), reporting passivation plateaus, Cl−-induced fluctuations, and Tafel-fitted parameters (Ecorr, Ef, Eb, ΔE, Ipass). 0Cu exhibits the widest passivation region and highest Eb. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Section 3.2.2, Fig. 6, Table 3) shows capacitive semicircles, with Nyquist diameter and Rct decreasing from 0Cu to 3Cu. Bode diagrams show the lowest impedance modulus for 3Cu. Mott-Schottky analysis (Fig. 7, Table 4) gives donor and acceptor densities that increase with Cu. Subsequent chunks must present immersion corrosion morphology, weight loss data (Figs. 8-9), SKPFM potential maps (Fig. 10), and integrate these with the electrochemical and carbide data to build a corrosion mechanism.
key_variables: polarization scan rate: 0.5 mV/s, range -1.2 to -1.0 V vs SCE
mechanisms: Cu addition promotes passivation but increases anodic dissolution and reduces passivation interval
higher Cu correlates with smaller Nyquist diameter and lower Rct, indicating faster corrosion
increasing Cu raises donor and acceptor densities in passive film, implying more defective film

The Mott-Schottky curves (Fig. 7) exhibit three regions with alternating semiconductor type: P-type in region I (-1 to -0.5 V), N-type in region II (-0.5 to -0.16 V), and a linear region III (-0.16 to 1 V) attributed to uniform donor distribution and slow electron motion. Donor and acceptor densities (ND, NA) were calculated using the Mott-Schottky equations with ε=15.6 and T=300 K, and their values are tabulated in Table 4. ND and NA increase with Cu content; 0Cu has the smallest ND and 3Cu the largest. According to the point defect model, higher ND indicates greater charge carrier density, more defects, and enhanced electrochemical activity. Consequently, 3Cu exhibits the highest sensitivity to pitting nucleation and Cl− ion adsorption. This chunk provides the semiconductor characteristics of the passive films and establishes ND as an indicator for pitting susceptibility, directly setting up the subsequent immersion corrosion and SKPFM correlation.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the immersion corrosion morphology and corrosion rate (Section 3.3), SKPFM Volta potential analysis (Section 3.4), and the initial part of the Discussion (4.1) including dislocation microstructure analysis and carbide characterization, as well as XPS surface oxide composition after immersion. Immersion tests in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 1–5 days showed that pitting severity increased with Cu content, with 3Cu exhibiting the most severe attack and grain boundary corrosion. The corrosion rate of 0Cu was 0.0546 mm/a, the lowest. SKPFM revealed that austenite has lower Volta potential than carbide-surrounding regions, and 3Cu showed a 1.2 mV grain boundary potential difference due to Cu segregation, driving microgalvanic corrosion. TEM confirmed FCC austenite matrix with κ-carbide precipitation, identified carbides as NbC enriched in Nb, C, W, Mo, and observed edge dislocations near carbides that promote localized pitting as high-energy anodic sites. Cu particle aggregation at grain boundaries was documented. XPS after 5 days immersion provided oxide film compositions: 3Cu oxide was dominated by MnO (38%), MnO2 (39%) and Al2O3 (16.3%), while 0Cu had higher Al2O3 (38.2%) and lower Mn oxides. These microstructure, potential, and oxide data directly feed the corrosion mechanism discussion in later chunks.

context_update.summary: Chunk 0007 concludes Section 4.1 with TEM/EDS evidence (Fig. 14) of Cu particle aggregation at grain boundaries in Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-xCu, and moves into Section 4.2 analyzing corrosion products and passivation films via XPS. Gibbs free energy values rationalize preferential Al oxidation. After 5-day immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl, all steels develop products containing Fe, Mn, Al, O. XPS peak assignments (Fig. 15) identify Fe3O4, Fe2O3, FeOOH, MnO, MnO2, Al2O3, CuO, Cu2O. Sensitivity factors (Fe 14.353, Mn 36, Al 0.56, Cu 26.513) are used to calculate oxide proportions (Fig. 16): Mn oxide fraction rises from 47% in 0Cu to 57.2% in 1Cu and 77% in 3Cu. Mechanistic explanations link Mn oxides to accelerated electron transfer, Cu-refined carbides to more corrosion sites in 1Cu, and grain-boundary Cu enrichment with synergistic carbide corrosion in 3Cu. Potentiostatic polarization at −0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 7200 s produces stable passive films (Fig. 17): 0Cu film is richer in Fe/Al oxides, while 3Cu film is unstable due to Cu-induced galvanic effects (Fig. 18). These results directly feed later corrosion mechanism discussions.

context_update.summary: This chunk (chunk_0008) presents the corrosion mechanism model (Section 4.3) and conclusions for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-xCu steels. It reports that Al dissolves preferentially due to its lower standard electrode potential (-0.447 V vs. SHE) than Fe and Mn, forming Al2O3 along with Fe2O3 and MnO. The Al2O3 content is highest in 0Cu (38.2%). Volta potential data (Fig. 10) show austenite matrix is less noble than carbides, initiating local corrosion.
